questions.stem: A 50-year-old man presents with severe headache, blurred vision, and confusion. Blood pressure is 220/130 mmHg. Fundoscopy shows bilateral papilloedema and arteriovenous nicking. There are no focal neurological signs. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The severe hypertension with papilloedema and encephalopathy is diagnostic of hypertensive emergency (hypertensive encephalopathy). Subarachnoid haemorrhage usually presents with thunderclap headache and focal signs; meningitis with fever and neck stiffness; migraine without such high BP and papilloedema; CVST often with headache and focal deficits but not typically with BP this high and papilloedema.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with known hypertension presents with acute onset breathlessness and orthopnoea. BP 200/110 mmHg. Chest examination reveals bilateral crackles up to the lung apices, and JVP is raised at 8 cm. Oxygen saturations 88% on air. ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of severe hypertension, acute pulmonary oedema, and LVH on ECG points to hypertensive emergency causing acute heart failure. Non-hypertensive HF would not typically have such high BP; asthma presents with wheeze and prolonged expiration; pneumonia with fever and consolidation; PE with pleuritic pain and DVT signs.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with central chest pain radiating to the left arm for 1 hour. BP 210/120 mmHg. ECG shows ST depression in V3-V6. High-sensitivity troponin is elevated. He has no prior history of ischaemic heart disease. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The severe hypertension with acute coronary syndrome (troponin rise, ST depression) indicates hypertensive emergency as the underlying cause. NSTEMI alone does not account for the BP of 210/120; aortic dissection would have tearing pain and pulse deficit; takotsubo usually has apical ballooning and emotional stress; pericarditis has diffuse ST elevation and PR depression.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 35 weeks gestation presents with epigastric pain and headache. BP 180/110 mmHg. Urine dipstick shows 3+ protein. Reflexes are brisk. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Severe hypertension with proteinuria in pregnancy is diagnostic of preeclampsia with severe features, a hypertensive emergency. HELLP syndrome includes haemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets; gestational hypertension has no proteinuria; acute fatty liver presents with hypoglycaemia and coagulopathy; chronic hypertension would be present before 20 weeks.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with poorly controlled hypertension presents with oliguria and confusion. BP 200/110 mmHg. Serum creatinine is 500 μmol/L, urine dip shows blood 2+ and protein 2+. Fundoscopy reveals arteriovenous nipping but no papilloedema. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Hypertensive emergency (malignant hypertension with renal involvement)
questions.explanation: The presentation of severe hypertension with acute kidney injury, proteinuria, and haematuria suggests malignant hypertension (hypertensive emergency). Glomerulonephritis often has normal BP or lower; TMA causes microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia; rhabdomyolysis has raised CK; obstruction would show hydronephrosis and normal BP.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a severe headache, blurred vision, and nausea. Her blood pressure is 230/135 mmHg. Fundoscopy reveals papilledema. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of hypertensive emergency?
options: Urine dipstick for protein and blood
questions.explanation: Fundoscopic examination is the key to confirming hypertensive retinopathy with papilledema, which is a hallmark of hypertensive encephalopathy. CT head is used to exclude intracranial haemorrhage, but the presence of papilledema on fundoscopy confirms end-organ damage in the context of severe hypertension.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of hypertension presents with acute dyspnoea and orthopnoea. His blood pressure is 200/120 mmHg, heart rate 110 bpm, and oxygen saturation 91% on room air. Auscultation reveals bilateral fine crepitations. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of hypertensive emergency?
questions.explanation: In this scenario, hypertensive emergency presenting as acute pulmonary oedema. A chest X-ray can rapidly confirm pulmonary oedema, which is a form of acute target organ damage. While echocardiogram assesses left ventricular function, it is not the first-line test for confirmation of acute pulmonary oedema.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man presents with sudden severe tearing chest pain radiating to the back, and a blood pressure of 190/100 mmHg in the right arm and 160/80 mmHg in the left arm. An urgent chest X-ray shows a widened mediastinum. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of hypertensive emergency?
questions.explanation: The chest X-ray findings and blood pressure discrepancy suggest aortic dissection, which is a hypertensive emergency. CT aortogram is the gold standard to confirm aortic dissection. Transthoracic echocardiogram can be used but has limited sensitivity for distal dissections.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with known hypertension presents with confusion and a blood pressure of 210/120 mmHg. Her serum creatinine is 200 µmol/L (baseline 90), and urine dipstick shows 2+ protein and 1+ blood. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm hypertensive encephalopathy as the cause of her symptoms?
questions.explanation: In hypertensive encephalopathy, MRI brain can show characteristic changes of posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES), such as white matter oedema in the occipital lobes. CT head is useful to exclude haemorrhage but less sensitive for PRES. MRI confirms the diagnosis by demonstrating reversible vasogenic oedema.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old primigravida at 34 weeks gestation presents with headache, epigastric pain, and a blood pressure of 175/110 mmHg. Urine dipstick shows 3+ protein. She has not had pre-existing hypertension. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of hypertensive emergency in this setting?
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, hypertensive emergency is usually due to severe pre-eclampsia. The diagnosis is confirmed by significant proteinuria, and urine protein:creatinine ratio (or 24-hour protein) is the standard test. Serum urate and LFTs support the diagnosis but are not as definitive. The dipstick is already positive; quantification is required.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with headache and visual disturbance. Blood pressure 230/140 mmHg. Fundoscopy shows bilateral papilloedema and retinal haemorrhages. Serum creatinine 180 µmol/L (elevated). ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Hypertensive emergency with papilloedema requires rapid controlled BP reduction. IV labetalol is first-line in UK (NICE) due to combined alpha and beta blockade. Oral nifedipine is contraindicated due to unpredictable hypotension. IV GTN is reserved for cardiac ischaemia/pulmonary oedema. Sodium nitroprusside is rarely used due to cyanide toxicity.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with hypertension (on amlodipine) presents with acute breathlessness. BP 200/110 mmHg. Chest X-ray shows bilateral alveolar shadowing consistent with pulmonary oedema. ECG shows sinus rhythm. Which of the following treatments is contraindicated in this patient?
questions.explanation: In acute pulmonary oedema secondary to hypertensive crisis, beta-blockers (labetalol) are contraindicated as they can impair cardiac output and exacerbate heart failure. IV GTN, furosemide, and hydralazine are used. Dobutamine is not first-line but not contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 28 weeks' gestation presents with severe headache and BP 170/110 mmHg. Urine dipstick shows protein 3+. She has no history of hypertension. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for her hypertension?
questions.explanation: In severe pre-eclampsia, IV labetalol is first-line for rapid BP reduction (NICE). Oral methyldopa is for chronic management. Oral nifedipine MR is also used but IV route preferred in acute severe hypertension. IV hydralazine is second-line. IV GTN not indicated.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with sudden severe chest pain radiating to the back, BP 200/120 mmHg, CT aortogram confirms acute type A aortic dissection. Which of the following treatments is contraindicated?
questions.explanation: In aortic dissection, reflex tachycardia caused by IV GTN is dangerous as it increases shear stress. The goal is to reduce heart rate and BP with beta-blockers first (labetalol or esmolol). Sodium nitroprusside is used cautiously with a beta-blocker. Morphine is for pain.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with known hypertension presents with right-sided weakness and dysphasia of 2 hours' duration. BP 210/110 mmHg. CT head shows acute ischaemic stroke. He is not eligible for thrombolysis. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for his hypertension?
questions.explanation: In acute ischaemic stroke with hypertensive emergency, IV labetalol is first-line to lower BP gradually (NICE guidelines). Oral agents are too slow; nifedipine is contraindicated; IV GTN may cause hypotension; hydralazine is second-line.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with severe headache, blurred vision, and confusion. Blood pressure is 240/130 mmHg. Fundoscopy reveals papilloedema and flame haemorrhages. ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy with strain pattern. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
questions.explanation: This is hypertensive emergency with encephalopathy. The immediate priority is to lower blood pressure safely with IV antihypertensives such as labetalol to prevent further end-organ damage. CT head is important but not before starting treatment. Oral nifedipine is too unpredictable; IV furosemide is not first-line; lumbar puncture is contraindicated with possible raised ICP.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 32 weeks gestation presents with headache, epigastric pain, and blood pressure 170/110 mmHg. Dipstick shows 3+ proteinuria. Platelet count is 80 x10^9/L. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
questions.explanation: This is severe pre-eclampsia with hypertensive emergency. The immediate step is to lower blood pressure with IV labetalol (or hydralazine) to reduce maternal risk. Oral nifedipine is slower; CT head not indicated without focal signs; delivery is needed but after stabilising BP; magnesium sulphate is for seizure prophylaxis but not the first action.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of hypertension presents with sudden tearing central chest pain radiating to the back. Blood pressure is 210/110 mmHg in the right arm and 180/90 mmHg in the left arm. ECG shows sinus tachycardia. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
questions.explanation: This is likely an aortic dissection. The immediate priority is to reduce both heart rate and blood pressure to decrease aortic wall stress. IV labetalol (a beta-blocker) is first-line; GTN alone does not reduce heart rate; CT is essential but not before starting antihypertensives; oral amlodipine is too slow; calling surgery is premature.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with palpitations, severe headache, diaphoresis, and blood pressure 260/150 mmHg. She has a history of similar episodes. On examination, heart rate is 120 bpm. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
questions.explanation: The presentation suggests phaeochromocytoma crisis. The immediate action is to give an alpha-blocker (phentolamine) first to prevent unopposed alpha stimulation if a beta-blocker is given. Labetalol (which has both alpha and beta effects) could be used but pure beta-blocker is dangerous; GTN is not specific; CT abdomen is for diagnosis later; oral propranolol is contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with known hypertension presents with sudden onset breathlessness and orthopnoea. Blood pressure is 230/120 mmHg. Examination reveals bilateral crackles and raised JVP. Chest X-ray shows pulmonary oedema. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
questions.explanation: This is hypertensive emergency with acute pulmonary oedema. The immediate priority is to reduce preload and afterload with IV GTN (nitrate) to relieve pulmonary congestion. Furosemide is also useful but GTN is faster acting; labetalol is less suitable in acute LVF; CPAP is supportive but not first; oral nifedipine is too slow.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with no known medical history presents to the emergency department with a severe headache and vomiting. His blood pressure is 210/130 mmHg. Which single finding on examination confirms that this is a hypertensive emergency?
questions.explanation: Papilloedema indicates optic disc swelling due to increased intracranial pressure from malignant hypertension, confirming hypertensive emergency. Cotton wool spots and flame haemorrhages can occur but are not diagnostic alone; papilloedema is the hallmark.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of hypertension presents with sudden onset of tearing chest pain radiating to the back. His blood pressure is 200/110 mmHg. Which physical finding is most specific for the diagnosis of acute aortic dissection?
options: Blood pressure difference >20 mmHg between arms
questions.explanation: A blood pressure difference >20 mmHg between arms suggests involvement of the aortic arch branches, highly specific for aortic dissection. Other signs may occur but are less specific.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with poorly controlled hypertension is brought in by her husband because of confusion and blurred vision. Her blood pressure is 230/130 mmHg. Which neurological symptom is most characteristic of hypertensive encephalopathy?
options: Sensory loss in a dermatomal distribution
questions.explanation: Cortical blindness (bilateral visual loss with intact pupillary reflexes) is a classic feature of posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES), which underlies hypertensive encephalopathy. Focal deficits suggest stroke.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with known hypertension presents with acute breathlessness. On examination, she has bibasilar crackles and a blood pressure of 220/110 mmHg. Which ECG finding would most strongly suggest that this is a hypertensive emergency with acute left ventricular failure?
options: Left ventricular hypertrophy with strain pattern
questions.explanation: In hypertensive emergency, acute pressure overload can cause left ventricular strain, seen on ECG as ST depression and T wave inversion in lateral leads with LVH. Strain pattern indicates acute decompensation. LVH alone is chronic.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with no prior history presents with palpitations, sweating, and severe headache. His blood pressure is 220/130 mmHg. Which of the following additional findings would most strongly suggest a phaeochromocytoma crisis rather than essential hypertensive emergency?
questions.explanation: Orthostatic hypotension is common in phaeochromocytoma due to volume contraction from chronic vasoconstriction and catecholamine excess, and is less typical in essential hypertension crisis. Fever and tachycardia are non-specific.
